coredata.dc:title: NIR-light controlled local delivery of nitric oxide based on self-targeting carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Na
Bai, Yan
Li, Yuan Fang
Zhan, Lei
Huang, Cheng Zhi
coredata.dc:description: Nitric oxide (NO)-based gas therapy has received ever-increasing attention in treatment of many diseases, especially cancer. However, the selective organelle targeted delivery and controllable release of NO remains highly desirable. In this work, a sequentially targeting delivery nanoplatform has been constructed by encapsulating heat-responsive S-nitrosocysteine (the NO donor, SNC) conjugated mitochondria-targeting carbon dots (MitoCDs) and indocyanine green (ICG) within liposomes which are further modified with aptamer that can specifically bind to nucleolin overexpressed on the surface of several cancer cells. MitoCDs were prepared via one-step hydrothermal reaction using citric acid and m-diethylaminophenol as starting materials and used without any external targeting molecules. ICG molecules were employed as NIR-triggered photothermal agent followed by NO precisely controlled release in mitochondria. By combining gas and photothermal treatment, the nanocarrier produced a synergistic and superior killing effect on several cancer cell lines. And the cell growth inhibition of NCL-CSI@Lip on HEp-2 cells raised up to 72.39 %. Such an ‘all-in-one’ mitochondria-targeted NO nanocarrier may lead to new way for overcoming the problem of multidrug resistance in intracellular drug delivery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Self-targeting carbon dots
Nucleolin aptamer
Synergistic effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sequentially-targeted ability for cancer cell killing

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 21976144 and No. 22134005), the Chongqing Talents Program for Outstanding Scientists (No.cstc2021ycjh-bgzxm0179).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterizations of the MitoCDs. (a–c) TEM and HRTEM image of MitoCDs. (d) The corresponding particle size distribution profile. (e) AFM image. (f) The corresponding height profile. (g) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of MitoCDs (inset: photograph of the MitoCDs solution under daylight and a 365 nm UV lamp). (h) Fluorescence emission spectra of MitoCDs at different excitation wavelengths.

Fluorescence colocalization of MitoCDs or MitoCDs-SNC conjugates with MitoTacker Green in HEp-2 cells. (a) Images of MitoTracker Green (200 nmol/L), (b) images of MitoCDs (50 μg/mL) or MitoCDs-SNC (50 μg/mL), (c) merged images, and (d) correlation plot of the MitoCDs or MitoCDs-SNC with MitoTracker Green. (e) Intensity profile of the blue line in (c). Scale bar = 20 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Characterization of CSI@Lip and NCL-CSI@Lip. (a) Hydrodynamic diameter distribution of CSI@Lip (inset: SEM image). (b) AFM image of CSI@Lip. (c) Hydrodynamic diameter distribution before and after NCL aptamer modification. (d) The ζ potentials of CSI@Lip and NCL-CSI@Lip. Error bars indicate standard deviations (n = 3).

Photothermal effect of CSI@Lip aqueous solution upon laser irradiation. (a) Temperature changes of MitoCDs, MitoCDs-SNC, CSI@Lip, and free ICG irradiated with 808 nm laser (0.33 W/cm2). (b) Temperature changes of CSI@Lip at different concentrations under irradiation. (c) Steady-state thermal efficiency curves of CSI@Lip. (d) Plot of cooling time vs negative natural logarithm of the temperature change obtained from (c). The concentrations of CSI@Lip in the figures are all expressed as equivalent ICG concentrations.

The generation of NO. (a) The NO release concentration from CSI@Lip with different treatments (dark, 50 °C water bath for 30 min, or 808 nm laser irradiation for 10 min). (b) The NO release concentration from different concentrations of CSI@Lip under 808 nm laser irradiation for 10 min. (c) Images of intracellular NO generation in HEp-2 cells treated with PBS only or NCL-CSI@Lip, NCL-CSI@Lip + laser, NCL-ICG@Lip + laser, NCL-CS@Lip + laser (0.33 W/cm2, 10 min). Scale bar = 30 μm. (d) Relative fluorescence intensity in (c). The intensity in the control group is defined as 1.0. Data are presented as mean ± standard deviation (n = 5).

Cellular uptake and subcellular localization of NCL-CSI@Lip. (a) Fluorescence images of HUVEC, L02, HeLa, HepG2, and HEp-2 cells treated with NCL-CSI@Lip for 12 h. (b) Fluorescence colocalization of NCL-CSI@Lip with MitoTacker Green in HEp-2 cells. The cells were first treated in the absence or presence of CCCP to investigate the staining patterns of NCL-CSI@Lip in low mitochondrial membrane potential. Scale bar = 20 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Cytotoxicity assay. (a) Cell viability of HEp-2 cells treated by NCL-CSI@Lip and NCL-ICG@Lip with different concentrations under 808 nm laser irradiation or in dark. (b) The live/dead stained images of HEp-2 cells treated with PBS only or NCL-CSI@Lip, NCL-CSI@Lip + Laser, NCL-ICG@Lip + Laser, NCL-SI@Lip + Laser, NCL-CS@Lip + Laser (0.33 W/cm2, 10 min). Scale bar = 150 μm. (c) Cell viability of A549, MCF-7, HeLa, HepG2, and HEp-2 cells treated by NCL-CSI@Lip with different concentrations under 808 nm laser irradiation. Error bars indicate standard deviations (n = 3). *P＜0.05, **P ＜0.01, compared with control group.

Construction of the NCL-CSI@Lip for sequentially-targeted delivery of NO to mitochondira. (a) Synthesis route of self-targeting CDs. (b) Schematic illustration of preparation process of NCL-CSI@Lip. (c) A schematic representation of NCL-CSI@Lip for cell-mitochondria-targeted NO delivery and controlled release of NO by NIR.

head.ce:title: NIR-light controlled local delivery of nitric oxide based on self-targeting carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Biomedical Analytics, Chongqing Science and Technology Bureau, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Biomedical Analytics, Chongqing Science and Technology Bureau, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Jishou University, Jishou, 416000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Jishou University, Jishou 416000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitric oxide (NO)-based gas therapy has received ever-increasing attention in treatment of many diseases, especially cancer. However, the selective organelle targeted delivery and controllable release of NO remains highly desirable. In this work, a sequentially targeting delivery nanoplatform has been constructed by encapsulating heat-responsive S-nitrosocysteine (the NO donor, SNC) conjugated mitochondria-targeting carbon dots (MitoCDs) and indocyanine green (ICG) within liposomes which are further modified with aptamer that can specifically bind to nucleolin overexpressed on the surface of several cancer cells. MitoCDs were prepared via one-step hydrothermal reaction using citric acid and m-diethylaminophenol as starting materials and used without any external targeting molecules. ICG molecules were employed as NIR-triggered photothermal agent followed by NO precisely controlled release in mitochondria. By combining gas and photothermal treatment, the nanocarrier produced a synergistic and superior killing effect on several cancer cell lines. And the cell growth inhibition of NCL-CSI@Lip on HEp-2 cells raised up to 72.39 %. Such an ‘all-in-one’ mitochondria-targeted NO nanocarrier may lead to new way for overcoming the problem of multidrug resistance in intracellular drug delivery.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitric oxide (NO), as an endogenous signaling molecule, has been believed to involved in multiple key physiological events, including vasodilation, immune response, neurotransmission, and wound healing [1–4]. The function of NO is highly dependent on its specific concentration, and excessive levels of NO induce a variety of adverse effects, e.g. the inhibition of mitochondrial enzymes activity, which can impair cellular metabolism and further be exploited therapeutically in biomedical applications [5]. However, the short half-life and high reactivity of NO greatly limit its clinical utility. Besides, too much NO spilled into the circulation can cause poison risks including blood pressure reduction and heart rate acceleration [6]. Various types of photoinducible NO donors (metal nitrosyl compounds) or caged NOs have been reported and utilized as potential pharmaceuticals or reagents for biological studies in order to enhance efficacy and minimize toxicity or off-target effects. In recent years, many attempts have been made to selectively deliver optimal amounts of NO donor to the tissues and cells of interest based on the rapid development of nanomedicine [7–9]. Moreover on-demand NO release can be generally activated by internal environmental factors including pH, glutathione, glucose, enzymes, or external stimuli like near-infrared (NIR) irradiation and ultrasound [10]. Unfortunately, the lack of acting site-targeting capability resulted in non-specific intracellular distribution accompanied by higher dosage requirement as well as unpredictable side effects [11]. Therefore, nanocarriers tailored for targeted delivery of NO to specific organelles within the desired cells could offer enhanced efficacy in treating various diseases, including cancer.

Mitochondria-targeted NO nanocarriers have recently attracted considerable attention in cancer cell killing. It is becoming clear that mitochondria not only participate in oxidative phosphorylation, but also perform important roles in metabolic signal transduction, cell death and transformation, and inflammation [12]. Dysfunction of mitochondria has been found to be involved in many common disorders, including neurological and cardiovascular diseases, obesity and diabetes, tumorigenesis, and metastasis [13]. Cancer cells' mitochondria have different functions from their normal counterparts, particularly in the higher transmembrane potential and the altered bioenergetics, making mitochondria emerge as an intriguing target for cancer therapy [14]. More importantly, NO in cancer cells can highly inhibit the mitochondrial respiration, open the mitochondrial permeability transition pore, and stimulate mitochondrial apoptosis [15]. Hence, development of mitochondria-targeted nanocarriers for NO delivery which exhibit high ability to mitigate off-target effects and prevent drug resistance is highly significant and critically needed. To achieve mitochondria localization, covalent linking of targeting compounds on the surface of nanomaterials is always necessary. There are many targeting molecules that can be used for mitochondria-targeted delivery such as rhodamine, octaarginine, quaternary ammonium salts, peptide, and triphenylphosphonium (TPP) [16]. Of these, TPP and mitochondria-penetrating peptides (MPPs)-based delivery nanosystems work very well in accumulating the prodrugs or conjugates to mitochondria owing to their biocompatibility and high uptake efficiency [17,18]. For instance, Wu et al. successfully constructed mitochondria-targeted and NO-based anticancer nanosystem taking TPP as targeting ligand [19,20]. By surface modification of MPP with supramolecular nanocarrier, Ji et al. proposed a nitric oxide (NO)-combined oxidative therapy that could specifically target towards mitochondria for orthotopic hepatoma treatment [21]. However, conjugating the targeting molecules onto the surface of nanomaterials generally suffer from complicated modification process and low binding efficiency, which could potentially prolong the preparation time and increase the cost in practical applications. Therefore, it is highly desirable to design biocompatible, stable, and robust nanomaterials with self-targeting ability for mitochondria.

Carbon dots (CDs), a promising and fascinating type of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, have become particularly attractive in various biomedical applications because of their small size, reliable biocompatibility, high excellent water solubility, and outstanding optical properties [22]. Interestingly, mitochondria-targeted CDs, especially self-targeting CDs without the introduction of any targeting ligands, have been developed for biosensing, bioimaging, and carrying the therapeutic agents. Previously, we presented a self-targeting CDs for visualizing of endogenous peroxynitrite (ONOO−) generated from mitochondria [23]. And Wu et al. reported that carbon quantum dots with intrinsic mitochondrial targeting ability can be used as carrier for efficient photodynamic treatment [24]. However, these targeted CDs are usually positively charged and appear to accumulate in mitochondria via electrostatic interaction owing to the distinctive negative membrane potential (−180 to −200 mV) of mitochondria. Depolarized or damaged mitochondria have decreased membrane potential, leading these CDs break away from the mitochondria [25]. It is still challenging to develop CDs located in mitochondria in a mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP)-independent manner for NO delivery. In addition, to further improve the therapeutic safety and efficiency, combination therapy has been regarded as a promising strategy.

Taken together, a novel sequentially-targeting nanoplatform which enables the precise and controlled release of NO at subcellular mitochondria to maximize cancer cell-killing activity was constructed in this work. As shown in Scheme 1a, CDs with self-targeting function toward mitochondria (MitoCDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and m-diethylaminophenol. As-prepared MitoCDs have mimic rhodamine like structure and possess strong mitochondria-targeting property even if the MMP changes. By integrating the NO donor S-nitrosocysteine (SNC) onto the surface of MitoCDs, MitoCDs-SNC can employ as controllable releasing nanocarriers for targeted delivery of NO to mitochondria. Furthermore, MitoCDs-SNC and indocyanine green (ICG) were encapsulated in a liposome, followed by the nucleolin aptamer conjugation which can assist the selective accumulation of liposome in cancer cells rather than normal cells. Upon irradiation with 808 nm laser, the ICG could be activated and triggered subsequent NO release through photothermal effect. As expected, this sequentially-targeted nanocarrier can effectively deliver NO specifically to the mitochondria of cancer cell. Remarkably, cancer cell lines such as HEp-2, HepG2, HeLa, A549, and MCF-7 has been significantly suppressed with synergic combinational treatment of NO and photothermal activity. The use of this nanoplatform have opened new avenues for delivering therapeutic agent to the disease sites.

1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), tert-butyl nitrite, and m-diethylaminophenol were acquired from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Citric acid, cholesterol, l-α-phosphatidylcholine (PC), and indocyanine green (ICG) were purchased from Macklin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) was purchased from Di Bai Chemical Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 1,2-Distearoyl-sn-glycerol-3-phosphoethanolamine-N-[methoxy (polyethylene glycol)-2000] (DSPE-PEG2000) was obtained from Ponsure Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). l-cysteine (L-Cys) was commercially available from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). NO assay kit (Griess Reagent), 3-amino-4-aminomethyl-2′,7′-difluorescein diacetate (DAF-FM DA), mitochondrial membrane potential assay kit with rhodamine 123 (Rho 123) were purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Calcein AM/PI double stain kit was acquired from Yeasen Biotechnologies Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Methanol (CH3OH), dichloromethane (CH2Cl2), and chloroform (CHCl3) were commercially available from Chongqing Chuandong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Chongqing, China). The nucleolin (NCL) aptamer was synthesized and purified by Sangon (Shanghai, China) and the sequence is as follow: 5′-GGTGGTGGTGGTTGTGGTGGTGGTGGTTTTTTTTTTTT-Cholesteryl-3'. All reagents were of analytical grade and used directly without further purification. The ultra-pure water (18.2 mΩ cm−1) obtained from Millipore purification system was used throughout this work.

The UV–vis absorption spectra in aqueous solution were obtained on a Hitachi U-3010 spectrophotometer and emission spectra of CDs were recorded by F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometers. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was conducted in air at room temperature using an AFM-Smart SPM (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France). The scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of liposomes were captured from S-4800 scanning electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan). High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images of MitoCDs were acquired with Tecnai G2 F30S-TWIN field emission transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). Elemental composition and functional group of MitoCDs were characterized by an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo, USA) and an IRPrestige-21 Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan), respectively. Zeta potentials and hydrodynamic diameter were performed with dynamic laser light scattering (ZEN3600, Malvern). The cell viability and NO release assay were measured using a microplate reader (Bio-Tek, USA). Cellular fluorescence images were taken by a DSU live-cell confocal microscope (Olympus, Japan) system.

MitoCDs were prepared following a typical hydrothermal route. In brief, citric acid (60 mg) and m-diethylaminophenol (25.8 mg) were dispersed into 4 mL of ultra-pure water. After being transferred into a 20 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, the solution was heated at 160 °C for 2 h. The products were cooled down to room temperature and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min to remove large particles. Subsequently, the supernatants were further purified by silica gel column chromatography using mixture of dichloromethane and methanol as an eluent. Afterwards, the MitoCDs were obtained through freeze-drying.

Firstly, the NO donor SNC was synthesized by the nitrosation reaction between L-Cys and tert-butyl nitrite as described previously [26]. Typically, 0.6 mM L-Cys and 100 μL tert-butyl nitrite were mixed in 10 mL of methanol/water solution (5:1) and the resulting reaction mixture was stirred at 20 °C under dark condition for 4 h to generate SNC. Meanwhile, to activate the carboxylate groups in MitoCDs, 19 mg EDC and 22 mg NHS were added to MitoCDs in 5 mL of 0.05 M MES buffer (pH 6.1) and stirred at room temperature for 30 min. SNC was introduced and reacted with the above mixture at room temperature overnight. The MitoCDs-SNC conjugates were collected by ultrafiltration and stored at 4 °C for further use.

The CSI@Lip was prepared by a classical thin-film hydration method [27]. Briefly, liposome components PC, cholesterol, DSPE-PEG2000, and ICG were dissolved in a chloroform/methanol solution at a molar ratio of 61: 29: 6: 3 and then transferred into a round-bottom flask. The resulting mixture was evaporated in a rotary evaporator under reduced pressure at 40 °C for 30 min, yielding a thin lipid film. Subsequently, the dried film was hydrated with 25 mM HEPES buffer (pH 7.4) which containing 20 mg MitoCDs-SNC with vigorous shaking at 37 °C for 30 min. To obtain desired liposome diameter, the liposome dispersion was extruded repeatedly through a 100 nm polycarbonate membrane using a mini liposome extruder (Avestin Inc., Canada), followed by removing excess ICG and MitoCDs-SNC by a 25 kDa dialysis membrane at 4 °C. Furthermore, the CSI@Lip solutions were incubated with NCL aptamer in HEPES buffer overnight at room temperature and purified additionally with dialysis membrane.

All cell lines including human lung adenocarcinoma cells (A549), cervical carcinoma cells (HeLa), breast carcinoma cells (MCF-7), liver carcinoma cells (HepG2), laryngeal carcinoma epithelial cells (HEp-2), normal human liver cells (L02), human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC) were purchased from the Xiangya School of Medicine of Central South University (Hunan, China). The cell lines MCF-7 and HeLa were grown in the high glucose DMEM medium containing 10 % (v/v) fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 100 units/mL penicillin/streptomycin. The A549 HepG2, HEp-2, HUVEC, and L02 cell lines were maintained in the medium of RPMI 1640 supplemented with 10 % (v/v) FBS and penicillin/streptomycin. All the cells were cultured in a humidified atmosphere of 5 % CO2 at 37 °C.

To evaluate biocompatibility, HEp-2 cells were inoculated into 96-well plates and incubated in complete medium overnight to reach the concentration of 1.0 × 104 cells per well. The cells were then treated with empty liposome, MitoCDs, SNC, and ICG at various concentrations. After co-incubation for 24 h, the original medium was replaced with a fresh medium containing CCK-8 solution to evaluate the cell viability after incubation for another 30 min. The absorbance of each well at 450 nm was recorded by a microplate reader. In addition, all liposome dispersions such as CS@Lip, SI@Lip, CSI@Lip, NCL-ICG@Lip, and NCL-CSI@Lip were incubated with the HEp-2 cells to evaluate the cytotoxicity. And NIR light irradiation was performed in above light-triggering-involved groups. Furthermore, other cell lines including A549, MCF-7, HeLa, and HepG2 were treated with NCL-CSI@Lip under 808 nm laser irradiation for investigating the cytotoxicity. Corresponding cell survival rates were measure by above mentioned CCK-8 assay. To visualize the cell cytotoxicity results, Calcein-AM/PI staining reagents were adopted to stain live and dead cells. HEp-2 cells (a seeding density of 1.0 × 105 cells per well) were incubated in 24-well culture plates with medium containing NCL-CSI@Lip with or without laser irradiation (0.33 W/cm2, 10 min) for 12 h, respectively. After co-incubation for another 12 h, the cells were trypsinized and collected by centrifugation. Then, Calcein-AM (2 μM) and PI (4.5 μM) mixture was introduced after the removal of the medium. The fluorescent images of these cells were acquired while HEp-2 cells were thoroughly rinsed with PBS buffer.

HEp-2 cells were seeded at a density of 1.0 × 105 cells per well and further cultured with MitoCDs for 2 h. For studying the localization of MitoCDs, MitoTracker Green (200 nM) was added to specifically stain the mitochondria as recommended by the manufacturer. Cell images were collected with confocal microscope, and colocalization was analyzed by the ImageJ software. Moreover, to evaluate if MitoCDs can locate mitochondria at low membrane potential, HEp-2 cells were first treated with carbonyl cyanide 3-chlorophenylhydrazone (CCCP, 10 μM) for 30 min and then incubated with MitoCDs (50 μg/mL) for 2 h and subsequently with MitoTracker Green (200 nM) for 30 min. Meanwhile, CCCP-stimulated cells were stained with Rho 123 (1 μM) for 30 min as a control group. The cells were washed with PBS three times before imaging.

In order to study the photothermal effect of the material, we added different concentrations of CSI@Lip solution into a removable 96-well plate, surrounded by foam filling to prevent heat dissipation. A laser with a wavelength of 808 nm (power density: 0.33 W/cm2) was used for irradiation, and at the same time a temperature probe was inserted into the solution to monitor the solution warming in real time, the temperature was recorded every 30 s, and the irradiation was continued for 10 min. In addition, an equal volume of water was made to be treated in the same way as a control.

NO released from different amounts of CSI@Lip under laser irradiation was evaluated using colorimetric assay kit. According to the manufacturer's instruction, Griess reagent I and Griess reagent II were added sequentially followed by incubation at room temperature for 15 min. The absorbance at 540 nm was then recorded using a microplate reader. The equivalent of NO released was further estimated using the calibration curve (0, 1, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80 and 100 μM).

The generation of NO in intracellular was measured with a fluorescent probe DAF-FM DA diacetate. Briefly, HEp-2 cells were seeded on confocal dish at a density of 1.0 × 105 cells/well. Next, the cells were incubated with fresh medium containing NCL-CSI@Lip for 12 h. After NIR laser irradiation, the cells were mixed with 5 μM DAF-FM DA diacetate for 30 min. Production of NO was then detected qualitatively under confocal microscope.

Rhodamine like structure such as Rh123, Rh19 have been verified exhibit considerable mitochondrial targeting property in living cells [28,29]. Therefore, citric acid and m-diethylaminophenol were utilized as the precursors to obtain the MitoCDs based on hydrothermal treatment. Citric acid, containing both carboxyl and hydroxyl group, is one of the most common carbon sources for fluorescent CDs synthesis. In addition, it can be served as an acid catalyst for Friedel-Crafts reaction with m-diethylaminophenol to generate the rhodamine like structure [30,31]. Through a series of complex processes (condensation, carbonization, and covalent reactions), MitoCDs with rhodamine like structure with excellent optical properties have been prepared in one-step.

TEM image demonstrates that the prepared MitoCDs are mostly spherical in shape and nearly monodisperse in aqueous solution (Fig. 1 a). HRTEM images reveal prominent crystal fringes at 0.21 nm and 0.34 nm lattice spacing, which is consistent with the interplanar spacing of graphene (100 facet) and the spacing between graphene layers (002 facet) (100) respectively, implying the ordered internal structure of MitoCDs (Fig. 1b and c). The size of the MitoCDs is distributed in the range from 2 to 4 nm with the average dimension of 3.2 nm (Fig. 1d). The average heights of the MitoCDs obtained by AFM is 1.5 nm (Fig. 1e and f), corresponding to an average graphite layer number of 5. The prepared MitoCDs are light violaceous under daylight but showed bright pink emission under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation. Meanwhile, the absorption and fluorescence spectra of MitoCDs were shown in Fig. 1g. MitoCDs exhibit a strong absorption band at about 550 nm as well as a sharp fluorescence peak at around 605 nm upon excitation can be observed, which is spectrally like rhodamine molecules. The fluorescence quantum yield of MitoCDs was calculated as 24.7 % by using quinine sulfate as reference. Fig. 1h shows that MitoCDs exhibit excitation-independent feature when excited by the wavelength varying from 440 nm to 535 nm. MitoCDs exhibit stable property with pH ranging from 2 to 12 and no effect on the fluorescence intensity even when the NaCl concentration was up to 500 mM (Figs. S1a and b). Additionally, the photostability of the MitoCDs was tested under 1 h continuous excitation. The fluorescence intensity of MitoCDs is almost unchanged (Fig. S1c). The excellent photostability of MitoCDs was further validated by exciting with NIR light of 808 nm. Result shows that the fluorescence emission spectrum of MitoCDs was the same as before laser irradiation (Fig. S1d).

The peaks presented at 1349 cm−1 (D band) and 1577 cm−1 (G band) in the Raman spectra of the MitoCDs are related to the vibration of disordered surfaces and sp
2 -bonded carbon atoms in graphite (Fig. S2a). The calculated intensity ratio I D/I G was 1.05, indicating the graphitic structure nature of the MitoCDs. In addition, FTIR analysis was employed to identify the surface functional groups on MitoCDs (Fig. S2b). Specifically, the stretching vibration emerge at 3421 cm−1 reveal that hydroxyl (-OH) groups are distributed on MitoCDs. And the characteristic peak at 1720 cm−1 and 1242 cm−1 are attributed to CO and C–O–C stretching vibration, respectively. The results of XPS survey display that the prepared CDs consist of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) elements with atomic ratio of 82.67, 3.74, and 13.59 %, respectively (Fig. S2c). Deconvoluting the C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s peaks indicate that the presence of CC, CO, CN, C–N–C, C–OH, and C–O–C (Fig. S2d−f). Based on the above results, it can be concluded that the successful formation of the rhodamine-liked structure of MitoCDs.

Furthermore, the mitochondrial targeting ability of MitoCDs was investigated by colocalization experiments in HEp-2 cells. A commercial mitochondrial stain MitoTracker Green, which localize to mitochondria regardless of MMP, was employed to assess whether MitoCDs can specifically target the mitochondria. As shown in Fig. 2 , the fluorescence of MitoCDs almost completely overlaps with that of MitoTracker Green (the Pearson's colocalization coefficient is 0.9380), strongly confirming that MitoCDs can efficiently accumulate in the mitochondria without any additional modification. It should be noted that even decreasing the MMP of mitochondria by CCCP (an oxidative phosphorylation uncoupler), the mitochondria stained with MitoCDs exhibited excellent fluorescent signals as well as the control group. The staining patterns of MitoCDs coincided with MitoTracker Green where high co-localization coefficient was acquired when MMP decreased. Different from MitoTracker Green, Rho 123 is an MMP-sensitive dye and the green fluorescence distributed in mitochondria is quenched significantly in a lower-MMP status (Fig. S3). The cytotoxicity of MitoCDs evaluated by CCK-8 assay shows that cell viability is dependent on the concentration of MitoCDs (Fig. S4). At concentration of 200 μg/mL, the cell viability keeps over 80 % even though the incubation time is 24 h, indicating low cytotoxicity of MitoCDs. All these results confirmed that MitoCDs can be utilized as a robust and powerful MMP-independent probe for mitochondria tracking.

The process for the preparation of NCL-CSI@Lip is outlined in Scheme 1b. First, SNC, as the thermal-sensitive NO donor, was synthesized based on the nitrosation reaction between L-Cys and tertbutyl nitrite. To check for NO release in aqueous solution, the levels of NO2 − resulting from NO autoxidation were evaluated by the standard Griess method. A linear relationship between the NO concentration and the colorimetric response can be obtained (Figs. S5a and b). Based on this, NO production from SNC is strongly temperature-dependent and that is increases with increasing temperature (Fig. S5c). Also, the NO release is in a time-dependent manner (Fig. S5d).

The formation of MitoCDs-SNC based on the EDC/NHS coupling reaction was further verified by the characteristic peak at 322 nm and 550 nm which is attributed to SNC and MitoCDs in the UV–vis spectrum (Fig. S6a). Dynamic light scattering measurements show the hydrodynamic diameter of MitoCDs-SNC is 38.3 nm, which is much larger than the hydrodynamic diameter of MitoCDs (5.8 nm), confirming that SNC has been successfully functionalized on MitoCDs (Fig. S6b). In addition, the Griess method was taken to assess the generation of NO from MitoCDs-SNC (Fig. S6c). The level of NO increased significantly when keeping MitoCDs-SNC aqueous solution at 75 °C, showing the functionalization does not affect the thermosensitive property of SNC. Notably, MitoCDs-SNC shows fluorescence emission peak at ∼602 nm, which did not change upon loading SNC (Fig. S6d). Subsequently, MitoCDs-SNC and ICG molecules were then loaded into the liposomes formed with PC, cholesterol, and DSPE-PEG via a thin-film hydration method. SEM and AFM images clearly show the prepared CSI@Lip conjugates are well-defined spherical shapes and uniform distribution (Fig. 3 a and b). Addition of a concentrated solution of Triton X-100 to these CSI@Lips result in the rapid liberation of MitoCDs-SNC (Fig. S7a). The strong fluorescence reveals that the MitoCDs-SNC has been successfully encapsulated into liposomes. Typical absorption peaks are observed at 736 nm and 802 nm in accordance with the absorption peaks of free ICG at 718 nm and 778 nm (Fig. S7b), confirming the successful loading of ICG into liposome. These redshifted absorption peaks could be due to the partially formed ICG dimers or oligomers (J-aggregates). Entrapment efficiency of MitoCDs and ICG in liposomes were calculated as 18.05 % and 26.65 %, respectively [32]. Furthermore, the diameter and ζ potential of prepared CSI@Lips were measured. As shown in Fig. 3c, the average hydrodynamic diameter of CSI@Lips is 98.55 nm and the average ζ potential is −5.13 mV (Fig. 3d). After reaction with NCL aptamer, the diameter of NCL-CSI@Lips increases to 107.7 nm and the ζ potential changes to −9.79 mV for the contribution of NCL binding.

ICG, an effective photothermal agent, can strongly absorb NIR light and convert it to thermal energy. However, application of ICG in clinical therapy is limited by several disadvantages, such as poor aqueous stability, easy self-aggregation, off-target effect, and rapid degradation [33]. Liposomes have been employed to entrap ICG in this work. To investigate the photothermal effect of CSI@Lip, a safe NIR laser power (0.33 W/cm2) that is permissible for skin exposure at 808 nm was taken. As depicted in Fig. 4 a, similar temperature increases are observed for the control groups (H2O, MitoCDs, MitoCDs-SNC). And the temperatures of the CSI@Lip solution and free ICG are rapidly increased with temperature increases (ΔT) of 23.4 and 17.6 °C at the same concentration, respectively, indicating the strong photothermal effect of ICG. On the other hand, higher temperature response of CSI@Lip than free ICG under irradiation may be due to the protection of phospholipid molecules which can improve the stability and avoid its degradation. Accordingly, ICG encapsulated in liposome has highly condensed concentration and can capture the excitation thermal radiation effectively, resulting in higher energy efficiency and lower heat dissipation [34]. Since the excellent photothermal performance of CSI@Lip, it can be employed as a promising photothermal agent. The temperature rise profile of CSI@Lip is in a concentration-dependent manner upon laser irradiation, and higher concentration results in better photothermal conversion (Fig. 4b).

To estimate the photothermal conversion efficiency, temperature changes of CSI@Lip aqueous solution (20 μg mL−1) were continuously exposed by 808 nm laser irradiation (0.33 W/cm2) until a steady-state temperature reached, at which the laser was turned off (Fig. 4c). The photothermal conversion efficiency of CSI@Lip was calculated to be 26.61 % (Fig. 4d), making it a qualified photothermal agent with high heat generation even at a very low laser power.

After validating the successful preparation of CSI@Lip, it was of great importance to investigate whether it was possible to trigger the NO release by laser irradiation. NO release behavior was investigated in PBS buffer solution at physiological temperature using Griess agent. As shown in Fig. 5 a, only a very small quantity of NO was freed from CSI@Lip without laser irradiation while remarkable NO release can be seen on laser irradiation for 10 min. It must be mentioned here that the controllable NO release realized by NIR laser is advantageous for the intracellular applications. For comparison, CSI@Lip solution was kept at 50 °C via a temperature-controlled water bath for 30 min. The NO release amount was significantly lower than that using laser irradiation. The distinct release behaviors might be due to the local temperature on the surface of CSI@Lip is much higher than the surrounding temperature, which can efficiently minimize the side-effects of NO [35,36]. The typical NO release profiles for CSI@Lip under NIR irradiation were recorded (Fig. S8). NO was gradually released from CSI@Lip because the high photothermal conversion efficiency can provide enough energy for the subsequent decomposition of SNC. Following continuous irradiation for 10 min, the concentration of NO released can reach to nearly 18.15 μmol/L with 1.0 mg/mL CSI@Lip (Fig. 5b), which is sufficient for cancer therapy [37].Notably, this nanoplatform exhibits better NO release efficiency in comparison with other NIR-mediated NO-releasing nanomaterials (Table S1). From Fig. 5b, it can be seen that the higher concentration of CSI@Lip, the more NO release, suggesting that NO release rate can be regulated by controlling the concentration and irradiation time. Furthermore, the intracellular production of NO was verified by a commercial NO imaging probe DAF FM-DA. As expected, strong green fluorescence of DAF FM-DA in NCL-CSI@Lip-treated group upon 808 nm laser irradiation were significantly higher than NCL-ICG@Lip and NCL-CS@Lip-treated groups, while no fluorescence signal was observed in NCL-CSI@Lip-treated group without laser irradiation (Fig. 5c and d). In addition, the NCL-CSI@Lip-treated group with laser irradiation slowly and persistently released NO after 2 h, which was much higher than that of the NCL-CSI@Lip-treated group without laser irradiation. NCL-CS@Lip-treated group exhibited limited NO release, which might be due to the slow photodegradation of SNC donor. Given the above observations, the heat produced from the CSI@Lip is conductive to trigger NO release upon NIR laser irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sequentially-targeted ability for cancer cell killing
ce:section.ce:para: To avoid undesired release of NO and enhance cancer cell killing, sequentially targeting delivery system from extracellularly to intracellularly is an effective strategy. Nanoplatform in this work contained MitoCDs, SNC, ICG, Lip and NCL aptamer. MitoCDs exhibit intrinsic mitochondrial imaging capability. NCL aptamer plays a critical role in searching target cells and ICG is used to induce a photothermal effect and control release of NO in mitochondria. MitoCDs, SNC, and ICG were encapsulated in Lip to enhance the permeability and retention effect. The cell viability and targeting ability were validated using many kinds of cancer cells. Live cell imaging experiment results confirm that NCL aptamer is important for cellular uptake (Fig. 6 a, Fig. S10). NCL aptamer is a target element for nucleolin, which is overexpressed on the membrane of tumor cells versus normal cells. HEp-2 cells incubated with NCL-CSI@Lip showed strong red fluorescence. Similar effective uptake was also found in HeLa and HepG2 cancer cells. In contrast, little fluorescence could be detected in normal L02 cells, suggesting that the excellent intracellular uptake efficiency in cancer cells is benefit form aptamer modification. Given the targeted specificity to cancer cells, MitoCDs-SNC can be released from the liposome and subsequently accumulated in the mitochondria (Fig. 6b). Clearly, the red fluorescence originating from the MitoCDs overlaps very well with the green signals from MitoTracker Green (the Pearson's colocalization coefficient is 0.9445), showing a high targeting efficiency to mitochondria. More importantly, it was noticed that the MitoCDs-SNC released from NCL-CSI@Lip still holds mitochondria targeting property which is independent of the MMP (Fig. 6b, Fig. S3). Overall, NCL-CSI@Lip can selectively deliver NO donor into mitochondria of cancer cells.

The potential cytotoxicities of free Lip, SNC, and ICG were further assessed. The results indicated that the Lip treatment alone had no influence on cell viability of HEp-2 cells even the concentration up to 2.5 mg/mL (Fig. S11a). Neither SNC nor ICG shows concentration-depend cell inhibition effect within a certain range (Figs. S11b and c). And the cell viability of CSI@Lip was kept above 80 % even at a concentration of 2.0 mg/mL. However, significantly increased levels of cell death were observed when combined with laser irradiation, mainly due to the photothermal effect and a sharp release of NO in mitochondria (Fig. S11d). Consistently, CS@Lip showed certain cytotoxic activity because of the slow release of NO in mitochondria, while SI@Lip have stronger inhibition performance that may benefit from the synergistic effects. Taken together, this strategy based on self-targeting MitoCDs which can specifically release NO in mitochondria is more effective in inducing cancer cell killing.

Next, we compared the cytotoxicity of NCL-CSI@Lip and NCL-ICG@Lip at equivalent ICG concentrations. As shown in Fig. 7 a, high doses of ICG loaded in liposomes significantly suppressed cell growth with the stimulation of NIR irradiation, displaying a dose-dependent cell killing effect. Without laser exposure, no significant cytotoxicity was observed in both NCL-CSI@Lip- and NCL-ICG@Lip-treated groups, even under the highest concentration of ICG. It is noteworthy that NCL-CSI@Lip exhibited superior inhibition effect compared to the NCL-ICG@Lip by combining laser-mediated NO release and photothermal activity. The cell viabilities were further identified by the Calcein-AM/PI double staining HEp-2 cell experiments. It is known that PI can easily penetrate dead or damage cells and bind to nucleic acid with bright red fluorescence. In comparison, Calcein-AM has the ability to enter viable cells and is converted to a green-fluorescent calcein with intracellular esterases. As seen in Fig. 7b, NCL-CSI@Lip-treated group without irradiation showed mostly green fluorescence in the whole field. In contrast, most cells treated by NCL-CSI@Lip with NIR light were stained with red fluorescence, implying the efficient killing of cancer cells. When the cells were incubated with the NCL-CS@Lip and exposed to NIR irradiation, a certain degree of red fluorescence was observed, and the cell survival rate was relatively high. It is worth pointing out that compared with NCL-CS@Lip, NCL-ICG@Lip and NCL-SI@Lip give rise to more cell deaths under the same experimental conditions, revealing that ICG play an important role in cell killing and the heating effect can significantly trigger NO release in mitochondria. These results agree well with the CCK-8 assay. Finally, in order to demonstrate whether the cell inhibition activity was only showed in HEp-2 cells, several cancer cell lines such as A549, MCF-7, HeLa, HepG2 were tested and cell growth inhibitions were also evident (Fig. 7c). In addition, In addition, the in vitro tumor penetration of NCL-CSI@Lip was further investigated (Fig. S12). NCL-CSI@Lip completely penetrated into the interior of the tumor cell spheroids after 24 h of incubation and showed damage to the shape of cell spheroids. Based on the above results, we can conclude that the proposed sequentially-targeted nanoplatform present excellent cancer cell killing ability and is anticipated to find wide applications in cancer treatment.

In summary, we have successfully obtained an MMP-independent mitochondrion-targeting CDs (MitoCDs) and further constructed a sequentially delivery nanoplatform by incorporation of heat-sensitive NO donor (SNC) and ICG into liposomes for efficient cancer cell killing. MitoCDs can precisely deliver SNC to mitochondria and NIR laser irradiation substantially stimulated the release of NO based on the photothermal effect of ICG. Meanwhile, an increase in the cell temperature can also lead to cell death. This cell-mitochondria targeted delivery system can damage mitochondria more effectively and display synergistic growth inhibitory effect on many kinds of cancer cells. Such an effective nanoplatform can provide a new insight for precise delivery and controllable release of anticancer agent.

Na Chen: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yan Bai: Writing – original draft, Validation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yuan Fang Li: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Lei Zhan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Cheng Zhi Huang: Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 21976144 and No. 22134005), the Chongqing Talents Program for Outstanding Scientists (No.cstc2021ycjh-bgzxm0179).
